bull_thesis:
Micron has converted a commodity-memory cyclical into an AI-memory franchise in real time, and the financials are validating every claim. In fiscal Q2 2026 (the quarter ended February 26, 2026), revenue came in at $23.9 billion — up 196% year-over-year and 75% quarter-over-quarter — marking the fourth consecutive quarterly record. Non-GAAP (non-generally-accepted-accounting-principles, meaning before certain non-cash or one-time charges) gross margin reached a company record of roughly 74.9%, with the following quarter guided to approximately 81%. Non-GAAP operating margin was roughly 69%, non-GAAP earnings per share were $12.20, and free cash flow set a record as well. The fiscal Q3 2026 quarter (reporting June 24, 2026) is guided to record revenue of $33.5 billion plus or minus $750 million and earnings per share of $19.15 plus or minus $0.40; street consensus sits above the guide at roughly $19.72 per share and $34.5 billion in revenue, suggesting management is setting a conservative bar into a rising cycle.

The structural change behind these numbers is that 2026 supply of high-bandwidth memory (HBM — the stacked memory chips that sit directly on AI accelerator packages) is fully committed under multi-year contracts spanning three to five years, a genuine departure from the quarterly spot pricing that historically governed memory markets. HBM4 36-gigabyte 12-layer stack chips entered high-volume production for Nvidia's Vera Rubin platform, and on June 1 and 2, 2026, Nvidia certified Micron as a qualified HBM4 supplier after it cleared the greater-than-10-gigabits-per-second performance bar. Data center revenue — Cloud Memory ($7.7 billion) plus Core Data Center ($5.7 billion) — now accounts for roughly 56% of total revenue.

The balance sheet is a fortress: net cash of roughly $5.8 billion (approximately $13.9 billion in cash against roughly $10.1 billion of debt), so the greater-than-$25-billion capital expenditure plan for fiscal 2026, which steps up further in fiscal 2027, is self-funded rather than debt-driven. Memory is a three-player oligopoly (Micron, SK Hynix, Samsung), and HBM is the direct physical beneficiary of every AI accelerator shipped — the amount of memory content per GPU rises with every new generation.

If HBM4 and HBM4e demand holds and the new supply capacity is absorbed by the market, the peak fiscal 2026 earnings run-rate implies roughly $70 to $76 annualized earnings per share, putting Micron at only roughly 15 to 16 times peak earnings with a net-cash balance sheet — inexpensive if the cycle proves durable. The key conditions are that AI capital spending absorbs the industry-wide capacity wave, that multi-year HBM contracts hold margins through the next supply ramp, and that Micron defends or grows its roughly 20 to 25% share of HBM production against Samsung's re-entry.
bear_thesis:
Three things are true simultaneously, and all three weigh against the stock — not the company. First, shares are up roughly 300% year to date and trade above the average analyst price target (roughly $945 versus a recent price of roughly $1,152), meaning the market has already acted on even the most optimistic forecasts. Second, every metric is at a simultaneous cyclical and AI-capital-spending peak: a gross margin of roughly 75% guided toward 81% is not a sustainable steady state for a memory producer — Micron's gross margin for the full fiscal year 2025 was roughly 39.8% and the company was loss-making in fiscal 2023. This is the top of an up-cycle. Third, the 'fully committed under multi-year contracts' claim is verified for 2026 and is an assumption beyond that.

The classic memory trap is being set in plain sight: Micron and its two rivals are each committing more than $25 billion per year of new capacity at the very peak of the cycle. The supply that breaks the next cycle is being poured now. Meanwhile, the HBM4 design-win catalyst has already occurred, and it fired against Micron's margin: on June 1 and 2, 2026, Nvidia certified all three suppliers — SK Hynix, Samsung, and Micron — for Vera Rubin HBM4, with supply-chain estimates attributing roughly 60 to 70% of allocation to SK Hynix, roughly 25 to 30% to Samsung, and 'the remainder' — the smallest share — to Micron. A three-supplier HBM4 market is precisely the structure that compresses the margins that are driving the current re-rating.

The investment thesis also has zero independence from the single shared risk factor: it is a pure, leveraged bet on AI capital spending durability, which accounts for roughly 40% of 2026 S&P 500 earnings growth. At a market capitalization of roughly $1.29 trillion on a company that earned $8.5 billion in fiscal 2025, the valuation prices peak-cycle margins as permanent. What would confirm the bear case: any pause in hyperscaler capital spending that re-commoditizes the spot DRAM and NAND memory book sitting beneath the HBM franchise; a single earnings call where management drops the words 'fully committed'; or HBM4e supply in 2027 to 2028 landing as a glut.
sop_bull: Bull-case fair value: $1,250 to $1,400 per share. This is a whole-company intrinsic value (enterprise value, meaning market cap plus net debt, measured against the company's earnings power) at peak or near-peak earnings, with no commodity haircut applied. The second-half fiscal 2026 run-rate implies roughly $70 to $76 annualized earnings per share; at a 17 to 18 times multiple — consistent with the re-rating thesis that multi-year HBM contracts have structurally de-commoditized the business, and in line with SK Hynix's re-rated roughly 17 times multiple and Goldman Sachs's 'storage PE valuation' argument — that implies roughly $1,200 to $1,370 on earnings alone. Net cash of roughly $5.8 billion (approximately $5 per share) and optionality from rising memory content per accelerator in each successive HBM generation add the top of the range. This scenario implicitly assumes peak margins persist two to three years and the supply wave is fully absorbed by demand.
sop_bear: Bear-case fair value: $520 to $720 per share. This is a through-cycle normalized intrinsic value. Memory businesses should be valued on mid-cycle earnings, not peak earnings: normalizing earnings per share to roughly $25 to $35 — well above the fiscal 2025 trough of roughly $7.50 but far below the roughly $70-plus peak run-rate, reflecting that HBM softens but does not abolish the cycle and that a three-supplier HBM4 market compresses margins — and applying a 15 to 18 times normalized multiple produces roughly $450 to $630; adding net cash of roughly $5 per share brings the range to roughly $520 to $720. The low end of roughly $520 reflects a 2027 to 2028 supply-glut scenario in which HBM margins mean-revert toward ordinary DRAM-commodity levels and the spot memory book turns negative. This range sits near the low-to-mid of the analyst price-target distribution (analyst low target: $510) and represents the relevant downside given that the current price already trades above the average target.
sop_breakdown: Reconciled whole-company intrinsic value. Probability-weighting the cycle: roughly 50% chance the multi-year-contract and HBM franchise partially holds (skewing toward the bull case of roughly $1,300) and roughly 50% chance the cycle normalizes into the 2027 to 2028 supply wave (skewing toward the bear case of roughly $600). Blending those scenarios produces roughly $850 on earnings power, plus roughly $5 per share of net cash. Cross-checked against sell-side consensus: the average analyst price target is roughly $945 (StockAnalysis $946, MarketBeat $913, Public.com $975) — our reconciled fair value sits modestly below the average target, which is itself below the roughly $1,152 current price. Every credible anchor — our sum-of-the-parts (valuing the business at an earnings-power multiple and adding the net cash separately) and the average street target — places fair value below the current price. The wide TipRanks average of $1,202 and high target of $1,750 are outliers reflecting peak-earnings extrapolation. Base-case fair value: roughly $820 to $900.
risk_reward: Unfavorable at a price of roughly $1,152. Upside to fair value is negative — roughly minus 22% to minus 28% (the price is above the base-case fair value of $820 to $900). Bull-case upside if peak margins prove durable: plus 9% to plus 22%, reaching $1,250 to $1,400. Downside if the cycle normalizes: minus 38% to minus 55%, toward $520 to $720. The asymmetry is inverted — modest, conditional upside against deep cyclical downside, with the price already through the average analyst target. New capital is poorly compensated for underwriting peak-margin durability at this entry.
catalyst_status: FIRED (HBM4 design win) + PENDING_HARD (June 24 print). The load-bearing HBM4-Nvidia-Vera-Rubin design win has FIRED and fired with a sting: on June 1-2, 2026 (GTC Taipei, Jensen Huang) Nvidia certified ALL THREE suppliers — SK Hynix, Samsung, Micron — for Vera Rubin HBM4 in full production, shipping Q3 2026, with Micron estimated at the SMALLEST allocation ('the remainder' after SK Hynix 60-70% / Samsung 25-30%). The re-rate from that win is spent and the competitive read is net-negative. The only PENDING_HARD catalyst is the fiscal Q3 2026 earnings print on June 24, 2026 (2 days out) — guided $33.5B / $19.15 EPS with consensus already above the guide; a beat is largely priced after a ~300% YTD run, making it an asymmetric DISAPPOINTMENT risk (sell-the-news) more than an upside catalyst.
peer_comps_note:
True peers within the scale-out memory and HBM layer (live-verified June 2026): SK Hynix (the HBM share leader at roughly 58 to 70%, near-term price-to-earnings re-rated to roughly 17 times, operating margin roughly 72%); Samsung Electronics (HBM share roughly 21 to 30%, re-entering HBM4, trades at a conglomerate discount and is viewed as undervalued relative to Micron); SanDisk (ticker SNDK, spun off from Western Digital in 2025, NAND-only, re-rated following the spin-off); Kioxia (NAND, exponential re-rating); and secondarily Western Digital and Seagate (hard disk drive manufacturers, adjacent storage).

On valuation: Micron screens inexpensive on peak forward earnings (roughly 15 to 16 times) but expensive on normalized or trailing-twelve-month generally-accepted-accounting-principles earnings, and is the only name in the peer group trading above its average analyst target after a roughly 300% year-to-date move; SK Hynix at roughly 17 times is the cohort re-rating benchmark and Samsung the value end of the range. On growth: Micron's year-over-year revenue increase of 196% is the steepest in the cohort, helped by a smaller base and rising HBM mix. On margins: Micron's operating margin of roughly 69% now rivals SK Hynix's roughly 72%, with both at cyclical peaks. On returns: all three suppliers are at peak return-on-invested-capital; Micron's net-cash balance sheet is the best in the group. On AI-capital-spending exposure: Micron is the most pure-play U.S.-listed HBM name and therefore the highest-beta and least-diversified exposure in the basket; SK Hynix has the dominant HBM franchise but lower accessibility for U.S. investors.

Summary ranking: Micron is first on growth and momentum, roughly second or third on HBM share, first on balance-sheet strength, but first (worst) on already-run-and-above-target valuation. The peer group re-rates together on AI capital spending — buying Micron adds no diversification against the HBM theme.
role_in_scaleout: Micron is one of three suppliers of high-bandwidth memory — the stacked memory chips that sit directly on the AI accelerator package and represent a hard physical bottleneck for every AI training and inference cluster. Its role is critical and its demand is durable through the HBM4 and HBM4e build-out, but Micron holds the third-largest production share in a three-player oligopoly, which means its specific franchise economics are less defended than the underlying theme is durable.
consensus_delta:
Where we disagree with the market: the street is not looking the wrong way on Micron — it is looking the right way and has already acted, which is precisely the problem. Consensus is Strong Buy (24 buy, 2 hold, 0 sell); consensus expects Micron to beat its own Q3 guide ($19.72 per share versus $19.15 guided); and yet the stock at roughly $1,152 trades above the average analyst price target of roughly $945. There is no expectations gap to exploit on the upside — if anything the inverse gap exists: the market is pricing Micron above where even the bullish sell-side will follow.

The one street assumption that is arguably wrong is the implicit extrapolation embedded in the current price — that peak gross margins of roughly 75 to 81% and 'fully committed' demand visibility persist beyond 2026. But that is a bull-camp error baked into the multiple, not a mispricing we can buy into. The HBM4 all-three-supplier certification (June 1 and 2) is information the consensus is under-weighting: a three-supplier Vera Rubin market with Micron holding the smallest allocation is structurally margin-dilutive, and the Strong-Buy chorus has not re-priced that fact. Net: where we disagree with the market points downward, not upward.
valley_of_death:
The nearest hard event is the June 24, 2026 fiscal Q3 earnings print — and after a roughly 300% year-to-date run with consensus already above the guide, the asymmetry favors a sell-the-news disappointment: an in-line or even modest-beat result can trigger mechanical profit-taking. There is no cash-burn pressure or near-term debt maturity to worry about (net cash of roughly $5.8 billion, negligible interest expense) — the near-term risk is entirely valuation and positioning, not solvency.

The real risky window is the 2027 to 2028 supply wave: the more than $25 billion per year of capital expenditure that Micron and its two rivals are committing now will land as new capacity in 2027 to 2028. The first sign of hyperscaler capital-spending digestion — named explicitly in the regime outlook as a tripwire: a hyperscaler capital-spending guide-down or an AI-monetization scare — would re-commoditize the spot DRAM and NAND book sitting beneath the HBM franchise and compress the peak margins on which the entire current price depends. Broader macro overlay: an oil-price re-escalation or an AI-capital-spending break triggers broad de-grossing across markets, and Micron — the highest-beta AI-capital-spending name in the basket — would lead any drawdown.
positioning_washout: There is no dividend-cut or yield-base forced-selling dynamic here (the yield is a negligible 0.04%). The forced-selling pressure risk runs the other way: Micron is a crowded momentum long held by AI-thematic and passive-flow investors after a roughly 300% run. It screens as a top contributor to index and technology-thematic vehicles, meaning a factor de-grossing event — the regime outlook flags that BofA Global Fund Manager Survey cash at 3.9% is a sell signal and that the top seven technology stocks account for roughly 30% of the S&P 500, leaving little dry powder to absorb a shock — forces mechanical, price-insensitive selling of exactly this kind of high-beta AI name. The shareholder base is exposed to the downside, not the upside: there is no large trapped short position to squeeze; there is a crowded long position to flush.
forcing_function:
The catalyst that forces the re-rating: two dated events, both already largely resolved or skewed to the downside. First, the HBM4 Vera Rubin certification — this occurred June 1 and 2, 2026, the re-rating is spent, and the competitive read is net-negative. Second, fiscal Q3 2026 earnings on June 24, 2026 (two days away), guided at $33.5 billion and $19.15 earnings per share with consensus above the guide — this is a sell-the-news risk more than an upside re-rating event.

The catalyst that would shatter the bull consensus is forward-looking and undated: the first quarter in which management stops saying 'fully committed' deeper into 2027, or the first HBM4e (2027 to 2028 ramp) pricing print that confirms the supply wave is arriving. There is no near-term catalyst that re-rates Micron upward from a price already above the average analyst target.
moderator_conclusion:
Micron is a genuinely excellent company at a genuinely poor entry point for new capital. The independent fact-checker scored it 5 out of 5 on narrative credibility — rightly so. There is no gap between management's story and the financials: fiscal Q2 2026 revenue up 196% year-over-year, gross margin roughly 75% guided toward 81%, and a net-cash balance sheet all corroborate every management claim, and the HBM franchise is real.

But the job here is timing and margin of safety, and on both counts this is a pass. Applying the current regime framework — which identifies a complacent melt-up environment where AI capital spending is the load-bearing wall and requires that every position have independent drivers of return — Micron is the single least independent name in the scale-out basket. It is a pure, leveraged bet on AI capital spending durability, the exact shared risk factor under the entire basket and roughly 40% of 2026 S&P 500 earnings growth.

The load-bearing HBM4 design-win catalyst has fired, and it fired against Micron's margin (Nvidia certified all three suppliers June 1 and 2, with Micron receiving the smallest Vera Rubin allocation) — a textbook case of a catalyst that has already run, is out of its timing window, and is margin-dilutive, all of which the methodology penalizes. The stock is up roughly 300% year to date and trades above its average analyst price target (roughly $945 versus roughly $1,152); our reconciled sum-of-the-parts fair value ($820 to $900) and the average street target both sit below the current price.

The risk/reward is inverted: modest, conditional upside (plus 9 to 22% only if peak margins prove permanent) against minus 38 to 55% cyclical downside. Valuation conviction is also low — even stripping away the catalyst timing overlay, the valuation versus fair value is unfavorable; this is a peak-earnings name priced through the cycle, the mirror image of a value trap (a quality trap at the top). The moat — the durable competitive advantage — is narrow (a three-player commodity oligopoly in which Micron holds the third-largest HBM share) and widening only temporarily via qualification lock-in; the secular (long-term structural) threat is manageable, since AI consumes memory rather than displacing it — the terminal risk is a cyclical glut, not obsolescence.

The right stance is to own the HBM theme through a more independent or less-extended expression and to watch Micron for a cyclical capitulation — a glut scare or a hyperscaler capital-spending-digestion flush — that resets the price toward normalized earnings power, rather than chasing a peak-margin name above the average analyst target two days before a likely sell-the-news earnings print.
interrogator_dossier:
Interrogator Forensic Dossier — Micron Technology (MU)

Prepared for the investment committee, scale-out Basket F (Memory), data live-verified June 22, 2026. Fiscal Q3 2026 reports June 24, 2026 (two days out); the most recent reported quarter is fiscal Q2 2026, ended February 26, 2026. Price roughly $1,152, market cap roughly $1.29 trillion, roughly 1.13 billion shares outstanding, up roughly 300% year to date.

Narrative Arc

Micron's story has undergone the most violent narrative transformation of any name in the scale-out universe. As recently as fiscal 2023, the story was about survival through a brutal memory down-cycle — under-loading factories, idling capacity, negative gross margins, conserving cash. By fiscal 2024 through early 2025 the narrative pivoted to 'AI is a different demand vector' — high-bandwidth memory introduced as a new structural leg layered on top of the commodity DRAM (dynamic random-access memory) and NAND flash cycle. By fiscal Q2 2026 the narrative has fully inverted from 'cyclical commodity survivor' to 'fully committed, contracted, AI-memory franchise.' Three concrete shifts: first, from spot to multi-year three-to-five-year supply agreements (2026 HBM described as fully committed); second, from 'we make DRAM' to 'we make the AI bottleneck' (HBM4 in volume production for Nvidia Vera Rubin, HBM4e ramp planned for 2027); third, capital expenditure re-acceleration (more than $25 billion in fiscal 2026, with a meaningful step-up in fiscal 2027). What was quietly de-emphasized: 'bit-cost reduction' language, now subordinate to 'mix shift to high-value HBM.'

Claims Versus Financials (Forensic Check)

For once the financials are richer than the narrative. 'Record revenue, fourth consecutive record' — verified: Q2 fiscal 2026 revenue $23.9 billion, up 196% year-over-year, up 75% quarter-over-quarter. 'Company-record gross margin' — verified: non-GAAP gross margin roughly 74.9 to 75% (versus roughly 39.8% for the full fiscal year 2025), Q3 guided roughly 81%. 'HBM fully committed for 2026 under multi-year contracts' — verified directionally (Cloud Memory $7.7 billion plus Core Data Center $5.7 billion equals roughly 56% data-center mix); the specific three-to-five-year contract claim is unauditable from the financials alone. 'Record free cash flow, up 77% versus prior record' — verified, though the full fiscal year 2025 free cash flow was only roughly $1.7 billion; the improvement is real and cycle-dependent. 'Meaningful fiscal 2027 capital expenditure step-up' — verified as stated, and it is the load-bearing risk: more than $25 billion committed against demand contracted for 2026 and assumed beyond. The forensic read: no gap between narrative and reality; the risk is that both the narrative and the financials sit at a cyclical and AI-capital-spending peak simultaneously — the question is not current-quarter honesty but the durability of the terminal earnings power.

Tone and Confidence Trajectory

Rising to near-euphoric, and appropriately backed by the numbers. The arc from down-cycle defensive language (cost, discipline, factory under-loading) to Q2 fiscal 2026 offensive language ('fully committed,' 'record,' 'multi-year,' 'structural shift') is the steepest confidence ramp in the basket. Guiding the following quarter up roughly 40% sequentially ($33.5 billion and $19.15 per share) is not a hedging posture. The surviving note of discipline is the explicit acknowledgment of the more-than-$25 billion capital expenditure plan.

Guidance Credibility

High. A multi-quarter guide-then-beat pattern; consensus for Q3 (roughly $19.72 per share and roughly $34.5 billion revenue) sits above management's $19.15 and $33.5 billion guide, suggesting management is sandbagging into a rising cycle. The important caveat is structural: memory guidance credibility is always high on the way up and collapses at the inflection, because management cannot see the turn. High credibility, conditional on the cycle holding.

Analyst Pressure Points

The questions analysts are pressing are not about the present — which is unarguably strong — but about durability: 'Is this cycle different?' (do multi-year contracts permanently de-commoditize the business, or do they merely delay the reckoning?); HBM pricing and competitive allocation (SK Hynix holds roughly 58% share, Samsung is re-entering — does a three-supplier HBM market compress the very margins driving the re-rating?); and capital expenditure timing versus demand (more than $25 billion at the peak floods the next trough). Management is not fully addressing what HBM gross margins look like once Samsung qualifies at Nvidia and the 2027 to 2028 HBM4e supply arrives simultaneously.

Red Flags and Green Flags

Positives: revenue up 196% year-over-year with gross margin reaching roughly 75% and guided toward 81%; a net-cash balance sheet (roughly $5.8 billion net cash, roughly $13.9 billion in cash against roughly $10.1 billion of debt); multi-year HBM contracts plus HBM4 Nvidia Vera Rubin volume production — a real design-win lock-in. Concerns: stock up roughly 300% year to date and trading above the average analyst target (roughly $945 versus roughly $1,152); more than $25 billion of fiscal 2026 capital expenditure stepping up further in fiscal 2027 — a peak-cycle capacity commitment; the thesis depends entirely on one factor (AI capital spending durability) with zero independence of return drivers; and memory remains a commodity at its core — one pause in hyperscaler spending re-commoditizes the spot book beneath the HBM franchise.

Hidden Signals

The segment re-cut (Cloud Memory, Core Data Center, Mobile and Client, Auto and Embedded) is itself a signal — management encouraging investors to value the data-center engine (roughly 56% of revenue) at a structural rather than commodity multiple, and it worked. 'Fully committed' is doing enormous work — true for 2026, an assumption for 2027 and beyond; watch whether future calls extend that language deeper into 2027 (bullish) or quietly stop using it (the tell). Capital expenditure language flipped from 'disciplined' to 'meaningful step-up' — historically a late-cycle marker in memory.

Capital-Allocation Assessment (for the Director)

Classification: strengthening operationally — but at a cyclical and narrative peak. No forensic deception; an honest, high-quality up-cycle. The single most important consideration: the entire return from here is a bet that AI capital spending absorbs the more-than-$25 billion per year of capacity Micron and its two rivals are committing, and that multi-year HBM contracts hold margins through the next supply wave — at a price (roughly $1,152) already above the average analyst target after a roughly 300% run. The fundamental case is strong; the expectations case is the problem.

Moat assessment: scale-cost advantages plus intangible assets (process and qualification know-how), with an emerging customer-switching-cost layer via multi-year HBM design-ins; moat width narrow (three-player oligopoly, Micron third-largest in HBM share); moat trend stable to widening during the current build-out but the product re-commoditizes on any demand pause. Secular (long-term structural) threat: manageable — AI consumes memory, it does not displace it; the terminal risk is cyclical (a glut), not secular (obsolescence). This is not a fear-driven value name; it is a peak-earnings growth name where the question is whether trough-normalized earnings power justifies the current price.

Management credibility 5 of 5; trajectory strengthening; moat narrow but real; moat trend widening; secular threat manageable.
skeptic_kill_fact: No single fact breaks the bear thesis — the adversarial stress-test confirms it. Every load-bearing bear fact verifies against primary and credible sources. First, fiscal Q2 2026 revenue of $23.9 billion, non-GAAP gross margin of roughly 75%, and non-GAAP earnings per share of $12.20 represent a cyclical and AI peak, not a steady state (full fiscal year 2025 non-GAAP gross margin was roughly 39.8%; the company was loss-making in fiscal 2023). Second, the HBM4 Vera Rubin catalyst fired on June 1 and 2, 2026, and fired against Micron: Nvidia certified all three suppliers — SK Hynix at roughly 60 to 70%, Samsung at roughly 25 to 30%, and Micron at 'the remainder,' the smallest allocation — which is structurally margin-dilutive. Third, the stock is up roughly 297 to 300% year to date, hit an all-time high of $1,149.43 on June 18, 2026, and trades above the average analyst target of roughly $945.6 across 44 analysts. The AI scale-out demand is already priced in, the catalyst is spent and past its timing window, and the entire residual upside is a leveraged bet on the single shared basket factor — AI capital spending durability. This is a high-quality company at an inverted risk/reward — exactly the pass rating the full debate reached.
skeptic_corrections: First, a date clarification: the dossier implies fiscal Q2 2026 was reported in February 2026; the earnings print was actually March 18, 2026 (the quarter ended February 26, 2026). This is immaterial to the thesis, and the attack arguing that the sum-of-the-parts valuation was built on pre-print financials fails — the financials cited are current. Second, the net-cash figure is unverified: the roughly $5.8 billion net-cash claim (roughly $13.9 billion in cash against roughly $10.1 billion of debt) could not be confirmed against the SEC press release (the SEC's website returned an access error); it is directionally credible and not load-bearing for the bear case (which rests on valuation and positioning, not solvency), but the precise figure should be re-checked against the 10-Q balance sheet before being cited as fact.
skeptic_evidence: SEC Form 8-K (Ex-99.1 press release), Micron Technology, fiscal Q2 2026 (quarter ended Feb 26 2026), filed ~Mar 18 2026: revenue $23.9B +196% YoY, GAAP gross margin 74.4% (vs 36.8% YoY), non-GAAP EPS $12.20; Q3 FY26 guide $33.5B +/- $750M, GM ~81%, non-GAAP EPS $19.15 +/- $0.40 — sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/0000723125/000072312526000004/

StreetInsider / Yahoo Finance / TechTimes, June 1-2 2026 (GTC Taipei): Nvidia certified Samsung, SK Hynix AND Micron for Vera Rubin HBM4 full production (Q3 2026 ship); supply-chain estimates SK Hynix ~60-70%, Samsung ~25-30%, Micron 'the remainder' (smallest allocation)
MarketBeat / StockAnalysis, June 2026: MU consensus Strong Buy, average analyst price target ~$945.6 (44 analysts) — BELOW spot; stock +~297-300% YTD; all-time high $1,149.43 on June 18 2026

TradingKey / IndMoney earnings preview, June 2026: fiscal Q3 2026 reports June 24 2026; consensus ~$19.72 EPS / ~$34.5-34.8B revenue sits ABOVE management's $19.15 / $33.5B guide (sell-the-news asymmetry after the run)

scale.role: The only US HBM supplier (HBM4 Vera-Rubin certified, smallest of three allocations); the memory toll on every AI accelerator.
scale.rationale: Genuine AI-memory franchise transformation (only US HBM supplier, HBM4 Vera-Rubin certified) — a real LEVER, but it remains a CYCLICAL at heart (certified as the SMALLEST of three HBM4 suppliers; DRAM/NAND cyclicality is the historic killer), which caps the franchise score below the durable tolls. Skeptic verdict CONFIRMED_WITH_CORRECTIONS, the correction is a date footnote (Q2 reported Mar-18, not Feb; net-cash figure unverified but not load-bearing), so a ~8pt haircut off a ~56 franchise. The June-24 print is a PENDING_HARD binary; base ~$820-900 is ~22-28% below spot (peak-margin risk, in valuation_posture); size small and wait.
scale.demand_durability: Medium-high now / cyclical structurally — HBM demand is capex-bolted, but DRAM/NAND cyclicality is the historic killer and the price embeds peak margins as durable.
